items.label: Capability overhang
items.notes: Useful for separating latent system ability from what product and policy currently permit.

items.label: Context budget
items.notes: Should connect prompt size, retrieval choices, and cost rather than treating context as free space.

items.label: Eval leakage
items.notes: Focus on test contamination and rehearsal rather than generic benchmark grumbling.

items.label: Safety debt
items.notes: Treat safety shortcuts as maintenance debt with operational consequences, not just policy prose.

items.label: Retrieval drift
items.notes: Different from raw data drift; target retrieval relevance and document freshness over time.

items.label: Autonomy tax
items.notes: Good for naming the overhead around supervision, recovery, and permissions once a workflow becomes agentic.

items.label: Approval theatre
items.notes: Aim at decorative approval steps that do little except reassure the slide deck.

items.label: Memory wall
items.notes: Useful for practical limits on persistent or reusable context rather than pure architecture nostalgia.

items.label: Human escalation
items.notes: Should be about when the workflow hands off to a person, not generic human-in-the-loop piety.

items.label: Agent reliability
items.notes: Focus on whether long-running agent workflows stay dependable beyond demos.

items.label: Tool reliability
items.notes: Useful for the ordinary but critical question of whether tools behave consistently when the model calls them.

items.label: Workflow drift
items.notes: Separate end-to-end agent or process drift from base-model behaviour drift.

items.label: Capability surface
items.notes: This should help distinguish what a system can touch from what marketing says it can do.

items.label: Demo fidelity gap
items.notes: Good for the distance between a polished demo and the production workflow it implies.

items.label: Orchestration debt
items.notes: Should describe the hidden cost of stitching together more steps, models, and tools than anyone can explain.

items.label: Prompt scaffold
items.notes: Useful for structured prompting that props up the workflow without pretending to be the workflow.

items.label: Governance stack
items.notes: A practical term for how controls, approvals, logs, and policies fit together around AI features.

items.label: Model rollback
items.notes: Focus on reversing model changes safely once production reality disagrees with release optimism.

items.label: Routing debt
items.notes: Useful for stacked routing logic that made sense incrementally and became unreadable collectively.

items.label: Exception budget
items.notes: Should name how many edge-case rescues a workflow can tolerate before the economics stop pretending.

items.label: Tool broker
items.notes: Useful for systems that decide which external capability an agent should see or call.

items.label: Agent observability
items.notes: Focus on seeing what an agent did, why it did it, which tools it touched, and where the workflow quietly became theatre.

items.label: Agent trace
items.notes: Useful for the recorded chain of model calls, tool calls, handoffs, approvals, and mistakes that made the final answer look inevitable.

items.label: Handoff policy
items.notes: Name the rules for when work moves between agents, tools, or humans instead of pretending delegation is a personality trait.

items.label: Delegation contract
items.notes: Should capture the scope, authority, context, and return format expected when one agent or person hands work to another.

items.label: Approval gate
items.notes: Useful for separating meaningful human control from the comforting button someone clicks after the decision has already escaped.

items.label: Permission prompt
items.notes: Focus on the little consent ritual that decides whether an agent may read, write, send, spend, or damage something real.

items.label: Tool call audit
items.notes: Good for the unglamorous record of which tool was called, with what arguments, by whom, and with what consequence.

items.label: Tool result
items.notes: Should distinguish a tool's returned data from the model's interpretation of it, which is where many tidy lies acquire formatting.

items.label: Structured content
items.notes: Useful for tool and protocol outputs that arrive as typed data rather than persuasive paragraphs wearing JSON perfume.

items.label: Resource link
items.notes: Focus on references to context that can be fetched or subscribed to, not pasted wholesale into the model like a ransom note.

items.label: MCP resource
items.notes: Should explain application-driven context exposed through MCP, distinct from model-controlled tools and prompt-shaped wishful thinking.

items.label: MCP prompt
items.notes: Good for reusable prompt templates exposed by a server, not evidence that the server now understands the business.

items.label: MCP roots
items.notes: Name the boundary of files or locations a client tells an MCP server it may treat as relevant, which is not the same as permission to eat the disk.

items.label: MCP registry
items.notes: Useful for catalogues of MCP servers and tools, plus the new procurement problem of deciding which helpful stranger may enter the building.

items.label: MCP sampling
items.notes: Focus on server-requested model generation through a client, where the direction of control is easy to summarise badly and implement worse.

items.label: MCP elicitation
items.notes: Name the protocol path for requesting user input during a workflow, not a licence for the agent to interrogate the office furniture.

items.label: Hosted MCP
items.notes: Useful for MCP servers run as managed infrastructure, where convenience arrives carrying authentication, tenancy, and vendor questions.

items.label: Prompt injection scanner
items.notes: Good for tools that look for hostile instructions in content, while not pretending pattern matching has repealed adversarial creativity.

items.label: Guardrail bypass
items.notes: Should describe the path around a safety control, especially when the control was sold as a wall and installed as a curtain.

items.label: Policy engine
items.notes: Focus on runtime rules that decide what a system may do, not the document repository where responsibility goes to rest.

items.label: Data exfiltration path
items.notes: Name the route by which sensitive data leaves through tools, logs, prompts, connectors, or a very helpful integration.

items.label: Context compaction
items.notes: Useful for summarising or compressing working state so a long session can continue without pretending nothing was lost.

items.label: Memory boundary
items.notes: Should mark what the system may remember, forget, retrieve, or leak, which is more useful than saying it has memory and smiling.

items.label: Session replay
items.notes: Focus on reconstructing what happened in a model or agent session, especially after the impressive bit becomes an incident report.

items.label: Reasoning budget
items.notes: Name the token, latency, cost, or policy limit on how much deliberation a model gets before everyone calls it decisive.

items.label: Planning budget
items.notes: Useful for the amount of exploration an agent is allowed before it must stop admiring the map and touch the work.

items.label: Capability escalation
items.notes: Should describe when a workflow gains stronger tools, wider access, or higher authority, not merely when the demo gets more confident.

items.label: Trust boundary
items.notes: Good for the line between systems, users, tools, and data sources where assumptions should stop and verification should begin.

items.label: Research agent
items.notes: Focus on systems that plan searches, inspect sources, and produce cited synthesis, not a chatbot that read three snippets and became a librarian.

items.label: Research plan
items.notes: Useful for the explicit search and source strategy before synthesis begins, which is where many confident reports quietly decide their answer.

items.label: Citation grounding
items.notes: Should separate citations that support the answer from citations that merely stand nearby wearing a tie.

items.label: Source triangulation
items.notes: Name the habit of checking a claim across independent sources instead of letting one agreeable page become reality.

items.label: Browser agent
items.notes: Focus on agents that operate through a browser surface, where clicking the wrong thing is not a metaphor.

items.label: Web task agent
items.notes: Useful for agents meant to complete web workflows end to end, including the dull parts that demos crop out for morale.

items.label: Action trace
items.notes: Good for the record of external actions taken by a system, especially when accountability needs more than a screenshot and optimism.

items.label: Computer-use agent
items.notes: Focus on agents that interact with graphical interfaces, where perception, permissions, timing, and irreversible clicks all become product requirements.

items.label: Screen grounding
items.notes: Name the link between what an agent sees on screen and the UI element it thinks it is acting on, a small gap with expensive hobbies.

items.label: Provenance signal
items.notes: Useful for markers that indicate where content came from, without pretending a signal is the same thing as proof.

items.label: Content credentials
items.notes: Should explain metadata used to carry origin and edit history for media, plus the awkward fact that metadata can have enemies.

items.label: Synthetic content label
items.notes: Focus on labels that disclose AI-generated media or text, and the policy habit of mistaking labels for literacy.

items.label: Watermark removal
items.notes: Name the obvious attack on provenance systems: removing or degrading the marker, then acting surprised that bad actors read documentation too.

items.label: Personhood proof
items.notes: Useful for attempts to prove a user is a real human in a world where bots are cheap and dignity is often an implementation detail.

items.label: Model Armor
items.notes: Treat as Google Cloud's prompt and response protection surface, not as magic chainmail for every possible model failure.

items.label: Golden set
items.notes: Good for a trusted evaluation set, while making clear that gold is still a metal people can overfit to.

items.label: Judge model
items.notes: Focus on a model used to score another model's outputs, which saves time right up until taste, bias, or leakage sends the invoice.

items.label: Red-team scenario
items.notes: Should name a concrete adversarial situation to test, not a dramatic meeting title with snacks.

items.label: Regression eval
items.notes: Useful for checks that catch old failures returning in new releases, the software equivalent of a familiar bad smell.

items.label: Eval dataset
items.notes: Focus on the examples used to test behaviour, including selection bias, freshness, contamination, and the temptation to worship a spreadsheet.

items.label: Eval coverage
items.notes: Name what the tests actually cover, which is often smaller than the confidence their dashboard has learned to project.

items.label: Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE)
items.notes: Useful for the embedded technical role that turns customer proximity into implementation leverage, and occasionally into very billable heroics.

items.label: Agentic commerce
items.notes: Names the shift from people browsing shops to agents comparing, negotiating, and buying on their behalf.

items.label: Agent wallet
items.notes: Useful for spending authority handed to software that can act across tools and payment rails.

items.label: Delegated authentication
items.notes: Covers how an assistant proves it is acting for a person without simply becoming that person.

items.label: Delegated spending
items.notes: Names the budget and accountability problem when agents can spend real money, not just produce suggestions.

items.label: Agent identity
items.notes: A practical term for giving non-human actors names, credentials, permissions, and audit trails.

items.label: Non-human identity
items.notes: Enterprise IAM now has to recognise bots, agents, services, and scripts as first-class risk carriers.

items.label: Agent seat
items.notes: A licensing term for when software becomes staff and vendors rediscover headcount pricing.

items.label: Tool-layer risk
items.notes: Captures the risk that moves from model output to what the connected tool can actually do.

items.label: Tool allowlist
items.notes: A crisp operational term for deciding which tools an agent may touch before the demo discovers the delete button.

items.label: Tool access review
items.notes: Governance that becomes useful only when someone checks real permissions, not declared intent.

items.label: Tool approval fatigue
items.notes: Names the human habit of approving repeated agent actions because vigilance does not scale as well as alerts.

items.label: Secure RAG
items.notes: Explain that retrieval security is permissions, provenance, and leakage control, not a checkbox on a sales slide.

items.label: Agentic RAG
items.notes: Retrieval that plans, retries, filters, and sometimes makes a larger mess with more confidence.

items.label: Retrieval permissions
items.notes: Names the access-control layer that decides whether the model should have found the document in the first place.

items.label: Context perimeter
items.notes: Useful for drawing the boundary around what can enter a prompt, memory, retrieval set, or agent workspace.

items.label: Semantic layer
items.notes: A fashionable enterprise term that can mean useful business meaning or a paid mist over messy data.

items.label: Vector sprawl
items.notes: Names the operational afterlife of every pilot leaving behind an embedding index nobody owns.

items.label: Enterprise RAG
items.notes: Promises institutional knowledge and usually discovers document hygiene.

items.label: RAG audit trail
items.notes: Covers source, retrieval, ranking, and answer evidence once an answer becomes a decision.

items.label: AI governance office
items.notes: The body created to make AI risk legible, ideally before it becomes a suggestion box with stationery.

items.label: Model risk management
items.notes: A banking and enterprise governance phrase moving into mainstream AI deployments, often with more policy than instrumentation.

items.label: Model inventory
items.notes: Before governing AI, firms must admit how many models, wrappers, endpoints, and spreadsheet miracles exist.

items.label: Eval marketplace
items.notes: Bought tests, benchmark packs, and the delicate trade in confidence.

items.label: Synthetic user research
items.notes: Asking simulated customers what real customers might have said if research were cheaper than humility.

items.label: Synthetic customer
items.notes: Generated personas used in testing, marketing, and strategy, where the customer is always available and suspiciously agreeable.

items.label: Voice clone
items.notes: Relevant across fraud, media, call centres, and consent; not just a party trick with better acoustics.

items.label: Deepfake consent
items.notes: A timely term for likeness, permission, revocation, and the policy problem of proving a yes was real.

items.label: Likeness rights
items.notes: Useful for creators, actors, influencers, and ordinary people whose face or voice has become training material with invoices attached.

items.label: Synthetic influencer
items.notes: The brand ambassador who cannot age, unionise, or remember scandals unless prompted.

items.label: Slop filter
items.notes: Names the defensive layer now needed because generation became cheaper than attention.

items.label: Slop firewall
items.notes: A filter for inboxes, feeds, search surfaces, and marketplaces trying to keep generated filler from becoming the default atmosphere.

items.label: Answer cart
items.notes: Fits AI shopping and search: a response that quietly turns recommendation into checkout flow.

items.label: Agentic SEO
items.notes: Search optimisation aimed at assistants, answer engines, and purchasing agents rather than human readers.

items.label: AI shopping agent
items.notes: A concrete consumer term with clear misuse potential: convenience, persuasion, affiliate incentives, and delegated buyer remorse.

items.label: AI app store
items.notes: Marketplaces of agents, tools, GPTs, skills, MCP servers, and other small boxes of implied trust.

items.label: MCP marketplace
items.notes: Tool servers become distribution objects and trust has to become more than a README badge.

items.label: MCP compliance
items.notes: Useful for puncturing claims that supporting a protocol automatically means safe, governed, or production-ready.

items.label: WebMCP
items.notes: A protocol-adjacent term for browser and website tool exposure moving into agent workflows.

items.label: Agent protocol stack
items.notes: The growing pile of MCP, agent-to-agent messaging, identity, permissions, and audit plumbing beneath the magic.

items.label: Agent sandbox leakage
items.notes: When isolation exists in the architecture diagram but secrets, files, network calls, or side effects find daylight.

items.label: Agent credential sprawl
items.notes: Every useful agent wants tokens, and every token wants to become archaeology.

items.label: Agentic IAM
items.notes: Identity and access management once agents act across systems rather than merely request permission politely.

items.label: Confidential inference
items.notes: For privacy-sensitive deployments where the question is not only what the model says, but who can see the input while it thinks.

items.label: Private inference
items.notes: A marketable phrase that needs careful separation between on-device, encrypted, isolated, and merely promised privacy.

items.label: GPU broker
items.notes: The middleman between ambition and available accelerators in a compute-constrained market.

items.label: AI data centre
items.notes: The physical footprint of intelligence: power, cooling, chips, debt, and local politics.

items.label: Power-constrained AI
items.notes: Names the reality that scaling intelligence now meets grid capacity, not just benchmark appetite.

items.label: Compute diplomacy
items.notes: National AI deals, chip access, data-centre investments, and strategic partnerships dressed as inevitability.

items.label: Sovereign cloud
items.notes: Pairs with sovereign AI while keeping focus on where models, data, control planes, and legal exposure actually live.
